glossary.definition: A process where each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster. This produces a grouping at the next higher level with one less cluster. The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)). Therefore, a pair of clusters is merged at each hierarchy level until one cluster with all data objects is acquired at the highest level. Figure 2 represents the process of agglomerative hierarchical clustering in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering.

Figure 2 represents the process of agglomerative hierarchical clustering in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering.

glossary.definition: A top-down clustering approach. It starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters. The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)). Finally, the clustering stops when each data object makes a cluster. Figure 4 represents the simplified idea of divisive hierarchical clustering: data objects having the largest distance from other data objects in a cluster are split in a separate cluster at each step of clustering.

glossary.definition: An output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset. According to (Hastie, 2017), “a dendrogram provides a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format”. A dendrogram consists of clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process. The key to interpreting a dendrogram is to focus on the height of clades, that is, the height at which two data objects are merged.

glossary.definition: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset. The coefficient values lie in a range [0,1], where 0 indicates that the model does not fit the data objects and 1 indicates the perfect fit. It represents to what extent the observed variance in the actual output values is explained by the features of the constructed model, for example, if the R2 coefficient of a model is 0.88, then approximately 88% of the observed variation in the actual output values can be explained by the model’s features.

glossary.definition: an example with its real classification label (the real output)
glossary.sentence: a training example with its real classification label (the real output) is given to the perceptron;

glossary.definition: the main steps of learning: initial weights are randomly selected, threshold value usually is set in the range [-0.5; 0.5 ]; a training example with its real classification label (the real output) is given to the perceptron; the perceptron processes it based on the summation and activation functions available in the processing unit; after receiving the output of the perceptron, the system checks if there is a difference between the predicted output and the real output; if the predicted output is different from the real output, then the perceptron adjusts the weights; otherwise, if the predicted output is the same as the actual output, then the adjustments of the weights are not made because the performance of the perceptron network is considered to be satisfactory; the perceptron continues the learning process (Steps 2-5) for other examples in the training set until some termination criterion is reached.
glossary.sentence: It means that the learning has the following main steps ( Negnevitsky , 2011): the initial weights are randomly selected, and the threshold value usually is set in the range [-0.5; 0.5 ]; a training example with its real classification label (the real output) is given to the perceptron; the perceptron processes it based on the summation and activation functions available in the processing unit; after receiving the output of the perceptron, the system checks if there is a difference between the predicted output and the real output; if the predicted output is different from the real output, then the perceptron adjusts the weights; otherwise, if the predicted output is the same as the actual output, then the adjustments of the weights are not made because the performance of the perceptron network is considered to be satisfactory; the perceptron continues the learning process (Steps 2-5) for other examples in the training set until some termination criterion is reached.

glossary.definition: the total classification error on all training examples reaches the pre-defined maximum permissible error or the pre-defined number of iterations is achieved
glossary.sentence: The learning can be stopped if the total classification error on all training examples reaches the pre-defined maximum permissible error or the pre-defined number of iterations is achieved.

glossary.definition: one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes; corresponds to the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset
glossary.sentence: An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes. Therefore, the number of epochs corresponds to the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset.

glossary.definition: the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples
glossary.sentence: The total error of the network is calculated as the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.

glossary.definition: a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a basic type of neural network that has greater processing power and can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: a layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features; neurons in the hidden layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour, and there is no obvious way to know what the desired output of the hidden layer should be
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features. Neurons in the hidden layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour, and there is no obvious way to know what the desired output of the hidden layer should be.

glossary.definition: the layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers; pre-defined by the number of input features
glossary.sentence: an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. The input layer is pre-defined by the number of input features.

glossary.definition: the layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons; pre-defined because the number of its processing units corresponds to the number of classification categories/classes
glossary.sentence: an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons; the output layer is also pre-defined because the number of its processing units corresponds to the number of classification categories/classes.

glossary.definition: computing neurons in the hidden layer; cannot be observed through input/output behaviour; no obvious way to know what the desired output of the hidden layer should be
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features. Neurons in the hidden layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour, and there is no obvious way to know what the desired output of the hidden layer should be.

glossary.definition: each neuron is connected to all neurons of the next layer; neurons at the same layer are not connected
glossary.sentence: Each neuron is connected to all neurons of the next layer. However, neurons at the same layer are not connected.

glossary.definition: each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: the calculation performed by each processing unit, including the bias input
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: the function applied by each processing unit after calculating the weighted sum
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: is based on the backpropagation algorithm
glossary.sentence: The learning in multilayer perceptron is based on the following steps (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): assigning initial random values to weights; selecting a training example from the training set; performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network; performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer; the training process continues until some termination criterion is achieved (see above). Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm .

glossary.definition: propagation of inputs forward in the network
glossary.sentence: performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network

glossary.definition: propagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer

glossary.definition: a difference between the right output and the predicted output
glossary.sentence: Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.

glossary.definition: an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system
glossary.sentence: It can be defined more precisely as an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system (Graham and Barrett, 1997).

glossary.definition: only knowledge relevant and important for tasks that the system will solve should be acquired and described
glossary.sentence: It specifies that only knowledge relevant and important for tasks that the system will solve should be acquired and described.

glossary.definition: knowledge should be described in a formal (mathematical) way with the aim of allowing the intelligent system to use and process it
glossary.sentence: knowledge should be described in a formal (mathematical) way with the aim of allowing the intelligent system to use and process it.

glossary.definition: an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: The structure of rules used by the human experts must be preserved in building a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the structure of rules used by the human experts must be preserved in building a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: The inference engine performs the control cycle "Recognize-act" (Luger, 2009) to link the rules given in the knowledge base with the facts provided in the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011). Thus, the inference engine carries out the reasoning by interpreting and applying the rules whereby the rule-based expert system reaches a solution (Hopgood, 2001; Negnevitsky , 2011). Therefore, a task of the inference engine is to relate production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support (Mallach, 1994).
glossary.sentence: The inference engine performs the control cycle "Recognize-act" (Luger, 2009) to link the rules given in the knowledge base with the facts provided in the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011). Thus, the inference engine carries out the reasoning by interpreting and applying the rules whereby the rule-based expert system reaches a solution (Hopgood, 2001; Negnevitsky , 2011). Therefore, a task of the inference engine is to relate production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support (Mallach, 1994).

glossary.definition: A system where the database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.
glossary.sentence: The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: Information that is stored in the knowledge base (implied by context: 'stores a representation not only of facts').
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them.

glossary.definition: The connections or links between the facts stored in the knowledge base (implied by context: 'stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them').
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them.

glossary.definition: Individual entities within a problem domain (e.g., John, Hugo and Mary).
glossary.sentence: there are three objects - John, Hugo and Mary.

glossary.definition: The connections between objects (e.g., Father-Son and Husband-Wife).
glossary.sentence: there are two relationships between them - Father-Son and Husband-Wife.

glossary.definition: The area or context in which a problem exists (e.g., the problem domain of human relationships).
glossary.sentence: in the problem domain of human relationships.

glossary.definition: The combination of information (facts) and guidelines (rules) that a domain expert possesses (e.g., deep knowledge (of both facts and rules)).
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules).

glossary.definition: Real domain experts solve problems in a specific domain.
glossary.sentence: Typically, the selection of a search direction is based on the analysis of how real domain experts solve problems in a specific domain.

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: An automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts.
glossary.sentence: An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts.

glossary.definition: Means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. (Categories include logical, networked, and structured representations).
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: Schemas that represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.

glossary.definition: Schemas that represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.

glossary.definition: Schemas that also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept.
glossary.sentence: structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept.

glossary.term: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: Schemas that represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: The theories developed in psychology that postulate knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts.
glossary.sentence: These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts.

glossary.term: Expert systems address a variety of problems
glossary.definition: The specific problems that expert systems are designed to solve (Interpretation, Prediction, Diagnosis, Design, Planning, Monitoring, Control).
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data. Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations. Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms. Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints. Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints. Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains). Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: knowledge is stored at the level of a concept it belongs to.
glossary.sentence: The researchers also have revealed that exceptional knowledge is stored at the level of a concept it belongs to.

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them.
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them.

glossary.definition: includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.

glossary.definition: a relation represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”).
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”).

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to.
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “Part of”. It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object.
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of”. It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties.
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties.

glossary.definition: a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.

glossary.definition: represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values.
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values.

glossary.definition: An essential feature of frame-based expert systems. The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame. Moreover, frames support overwriting of the inherited values.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems. The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame. Moreover, frames support overwriting of the inherited values.

glossary.definition: knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts.
glossary.sentence: they represent only declarative knowledge (knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts );

glossary.definition: A remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary. Also defined as "a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation".
glossary.sentence: When one encounters a new situation (or makes a substantial change in one's view of the present problem) one selects from memory a structure called a Frame. This is a remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary.” Also defined as “a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation”.

glossary.definition: Properties of objects/concepts. Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.
glossary.sentence: Slots are properties of objects/concepts. Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value.

glossary.definition: A frame that contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts. The class frames help us to represent a problem in an abstract form.
glossary.sentence: A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts. The class frames help us to represent a problem in an abstract form.

glossary.definition: A frame that represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain. Typically the instance frames include all slot values and are derived from the class/sub-class frames.
glossary.sentence: An instance frame. It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain. Typically the instance frames include all slot values and are derived from the class/sub-class frames.

glossary.definition: Relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.
glossary.sentence: Generalization relation. It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.

glossary.definition: Allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts. Frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole.
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation. It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts. Frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole.

glossary.definition: Allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation. It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle.

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system.

glossary.definition: Implement actions in a frame-based expert system.
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system.

glossary.definition: A procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994). Thus, demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009). Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames. Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.
glossary.sentence: A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994). Thus, demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009). Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames. Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: A value associated with a slot.
glossary.sentence: Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994).

glossary.definition: It is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it.
glossary.sentence: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;

glossary.definition: It is triggered when a slot value changes.
glossary.sentence: IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes;

glossary.definition: It is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame.
glossary.sentence: IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame;

glossary.definition: It is triggered when a slot value is deleted.
glossary.sentence: IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted.

glossary.definition: The inference engine of frames is based on the following principles: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, find a corresponding frame and check its slots. If necessary knowledge is available in the object frame, return it; if necessary knowledge is not found, then the inference engine can apply the steps specified below and stop when the necessary knowledge is found: perform inheritance (if it is possible ); check inherited demons IF-NEEDED. If they are, trigger them and output the results; explore the local relations with other frames and try to get knowledge from them. Thus, the inference engine searches for the specified property of the object until its value is obtained ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: The inference engine of frames is based on the following principles: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, find a corresponding frame and check its slots. If necessary knowledge is available in the object frame, return it; if necessary knowledge is not found, then the inference engine can apply the steps specified below and stop when the necessary knowledge is found: perform inheritance (if it is possible ); check inherited demons IF-NEEDED. If they are, trigger them and output the results; explore the local relations with other frames and try to get knowledge from them. Thus, the inference engine searches for the specified property of the object until its value is obtained ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects; frames support inheritance; frames allow adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values and, therefore, ensure the knowledge base's integrity. Frames also have disadvantages: frames are more suitable for representing static, not dynamic knowledge about objects/ concepts; frames do not have well-defined semantics; frames cannot represent some types of knowledge, for example, negation (Tom does not eat Mouses), disjunction (John eats eggs or carrot for breakfast), several/all/some/either, etc.
glossary.sentence: Frames have the following advantages: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects; frames support inheritance; frames allow adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values and, therefore, ensure the knowledge base's integrity. Frames also have disadvantages: frames are more suitable for representing static, not dynamic knowledge about objects/ concepts; frames do not have well-defined semantics; frames cannot represent some types of knowledge, for example, negation (Tom does not eat Mouses), disjunction (John eats eggs or carrot for breakfast), several/all/some/either, etc.

glossary.definition: A production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). A production rule is presented in the following way (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion Regarding the parts of condition and consequence, it is worst to remember that ( Negnevitsky , 2011): a rule can have multiple conditions and consequences joined by the logic operators AND, OR or a combination of both; the parts of condition and consequence usually incorporates two parts: an object (linguistic object) and its value. An operator (such as is, are, is not, are not, mathematical operators) links the object and its value. According to (Winston, 1992), if statements are added to the THEN part of rules, then a deductive intelligent system is acquired; however, if the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired. There are two examples of production rules given below: IF customer_age > 30 THEN discount =10 IF size is big AND colour is brown THEN animal is bear which include objects – customer_age , discount, size, colour and animal; operators - >, = and “is ”; objects’ values – 30, 10, big, brown and bear.
glossary.sentence: A production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). A production rule is presented in the following way (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion Regarding the parts of condition and consequence, it is worst to remember that ( Negnevitsky , 2011): a rule can have multiple conditions and consequences joined by the logic operators AND, OR or a combination of both; the parts of condition and consequence usually incorporates two parts: an object (linguistic object) and its value. An operator (such as is, are, is not, are not, mathematical operators) links the object and its value. According to (Winston, 1992), if statements are added to the THEN part of rules, then a deductive intelligent system is acquired; however, if the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired. There are two examples of production rules given below: IF customer_age > 30 THEN discount =10 IF size is big AND colour is brown THEN animal is bear which include objects – customer_age , discount, size, colour and animal; operators - >, = and “is ”; objects’ values – 30, 10, big, brown and bear.

glossary.definition: The working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009). The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: The working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009). The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result. However, to accept the system’s recommendation, the human expert should be aware of how a particular conclusion was reached, what was the line of reasoning and whether it is valid or not (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). Thus, an expert system should explain its reasoning and justify the output (Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result. However, to accept the system’s recommendation, the human expert should be aware of how a particular conclusion was reached, what was the line of reasoning and whether it is valid or not (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). Thus, an expert system should explain its reasoning and justify the output (Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The control cycle “Recognize-act” makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: The control cycle “Recognize-act” makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred (Negnevitsky , 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001).
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred (Negnevitsky , 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: In backward chaining, an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it (Negnevitsky , 2011). Backward chaining corresponds to a decomposition of the problem’s goal into simpler subgoals; thus, backward search in an expert system corresponds roughly to the process of hypothesis testing in human problem solving (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining, an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it (Negnevitsky , 2011). Backward chaining corresponds to a decomposition of the problem’s goal into simpler subgoals; thus, backward search in an expert system corresponds roughly to the process of hypothesis testing in human problem solving (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Chosen if a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred.
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: Chosen if a domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it.
glossary.sentence: However, if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: Use conditions specified in rules in a certain order and also apply rules in a certain order. Such an approach determines the conditions for successful problem solving.
glossary.sentence: It is well known that problem domain experts use conditions specified in rules in a certain order and also apply rules in a certain order. Such an approach determines the conditions for successful problem solving.

glossary.definition: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.
glossary.sentence: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.

glossary.definition: Both the structure and the sequence of production rules are significant in the context of a chosen conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Both the structure and the sequence of production rules are significant in the context of a chosen conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: This method is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one.
glossary.sentence: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one.

glossary.definition: A rule is more specific than another if it has more conditions: IF a THEN c IF a AND b THEN c more specific rule fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based expert systems have the following advantages: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...” ( Negnevitsky , 2011 ); Uniform structure. Production rules offer a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge; thus, the very syntax of production rules enables them to be self-documented ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Rules have no syntactic interactions. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not “call” another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules.
glossary.sentence: Modularity of production rules. Rules have no syntactic interactions. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not “call” another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules.
